questions.promptTex: \text{A right triangle has legs } 6 \text{ and } 8. \text{ What is the hypotenuse?}
questions.explanation: $c^2 = 6^2 + 8^2 = 36 + 64 = 100 \Rightarrow c = 10$. (This is the 6-8-10 triangle — a scaled 3-4-5.)

questions.promptTex: \text{A right triangle has hypotenuse } 13 \text{ and one leg } 5. \text{ Find the other leg.}
\text{The diagonal of a square with side 6 is:}
hints: The diagonal divides the square into two right triangles.
What's special about the theorem's setup?
It uses 'hypotenuse' — a term unique to right triangles.
questions.explanation: $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$ holds **only for right triangles**. For other triangles, use the law of cosines (a generalization).

questions.promptTex: \text{A right triangle has legs } 9 \text{ and } 12. \text{ Find the hypotenuse.}
\text{A right triangle has hypotenuse } 13 \text{ and one leg } 5. \\ \text{Find the other leg.}

steps: The distance formula is just the Pythagorean theorem applied to $\Delta x$ and $\Delta y$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Find the diagonal of a rectangle with sides } 8 \text{ and } 15.
hints: Diagonal of a rectangle is the hypotenuse of a right triangle with the sides as legs.
steps: A diagonal of a rectangle splits it into two right triangles.

questions.promptTex: \text{A } 13\text{-foot ladder leans against a wall, with its base } 5 \text{ ft} \\ \text{from the wall. How high up the wall does it reach?}
hints: Ladder = hypotenuse, ground = one leg, wall height = other leg.
steps: Set up the right triangle: ladder = hyp $= 13$, ground = adj $= 5$, wall = opp $= h$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Find the perimeter of an isosceles right triangle with legs of length } 6. \\ \text{(Round to 2 decimals.)}
steps: An isosceles right triangle has 2 equal legs and a hypotenuse.

questions.promptTex: \text{Which set of three numbers is a Pythagorean triple?}
hints: A triple satisfies $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$ exactly.
steps: Test each: largest must be the hypotenuse.

questions.promptTex: \text{Find the length of the space diagonal of a rectangular box with} \\ \text{dimensions } 3 \text{ × } 4 \text{ × } 12.
steps: **3D Pythagorean theorem:** for a box with edges $a, b, c$, the space diagonal is $d = \sqrt{a^2 + b^2 + c^2}$.
(Derivation: first find the floor diagonal $= \sqrt{a^2 + b^2}$, then apply Pythagorean again with that and the height $c$.)

questions.promptTex: \text{A right triangle has legs of length } 9 \text{ and } 12. \text{ Find the hypotenuse.}

steps.text: **Draw the triangle and label the sides.** Two legs ($9$ and $12$) meet at the right angle; the hypotenuse ($c$) is opposite that right angle.

steps.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><polygon points='40,150 200,150 40,30' fill='#fef3c7' stroke='#1e293b' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='40' y='140' width='10' height='10' fill='none' stroke='#1e293b' stroke-width='1.5'/><text x='120' y='168' font-size='14' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='600'>12</text><text x='22' y='92' font-size='14' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='600'>9</text><text x='130' y='80' font-size='14' fill='#dc2626' font-weight='700' transform='rotate(-37 130 80)'>c = ?</text></svg>

steps.text: **The Pythagorean theorem** says the squares built on the legs add up to the square built on the hypotenuse:
Geometrically: the two small colored squares' area equals the big square's area.

steps.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 280 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><polygon points='100,140 220,140 100,80' fill='#fef3c7' stroke='#1e293b' stroke-width='1.5'/><rect x='100' y='130' width='10' height='10' fill='none' stroke='#1e293b' stroke-width='1.2'/><rect x='100' y='140' width='120' height='60' fill='#bfdbfe' stroke='#1e3a8a' stroke-width='1.2'/><text x='160' y='176' font-size='13' text-anchor='middle' fill='#1e3a8a' font-weight='700'>b² = 144</text><rect x='40' y='80' width='60' height='60' fill='#fecaca' stroke='#7f1d1d' stroke-width='1.2'/><text x='70' y='114' font-size='13' text-anchor='middle' fill='#7f1d1d' font-weight='700'>a² = 81</text><polygon points='100,80 220,140 154,206 34,146' fill='#bbf7d0' stroke='#14532d' stroke-width='1.2' opacity='0.85'/><text x='128' y='168' font-size='13' text-anchor='middle' fill='#14532d' font-weight='700'>c² = ?</text></svg>

questions.promptTex: \text{A right triangle has hypotenuse } 13 \text{ and one leg } 5. \text{ Find the other leg.}
hints: You're solving for a leg, not the hypotenuse. Rearrange $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$ for the missing leg.

steps.text: **Identify what's known.** The hypotenuse is the *longest* side (opposite the right angle) — here it's 13. One leg is 5. We want the other leg, $b$.

steps.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><polygon points='40,150 200,150 40,30' fill='#fef3c7' stroke='#1e293b' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='40' y='140' width='10' height='10' fill='none' stroke='#1e293b' stroke-width='1.5'/><text x='120' y='168' font-size='14' text-anchor='middle' fill='#dc2626' font-weight='700'>b = ?</text><text x='22' y='92' font-size='14' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='600'>5</text><text x='130' y='80' font-size='14' fill='#1e3a8a' font-weight='700' transform='rotate(-37 130 80)'>13 (hyp)</text></svg>

steps.text: **Set up Pythagoras and solve for $b$.** The leg-squared equals hypotenuse-squared minus the other leg-squared:
(This is the famous 5-12-13 Pythagorean triple. Memorize a few — 3-4-5, 5-12-13, 8-15-17 — and you'll spot them all over geometry.)

questions.promptTex: \text{A 25 ft ladder leans against a wall with its base 7 ft from the wall.} \\ \text{How high up the wall does the ladder reach?}
hints: Ladder = hypotenuse (25). Distance from wall = one leg (7). Height = other leg.

steps.text: **Sketch the scene.** Ladder leans on wall: wall is vertical, ground is horizontal — that's the right angle. The ladder itself is the slanted side, which is the hypotenuse.

steps.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='30' y1='160' x2='220' y2='160' stroke='#92400e' stroke-width='3'/><line x1='180' y1='160' x2='180' y2='30' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='3'/><line x1='60' y1='160' x2='180' y2='40' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='3'/><rect x='170' y='150' width='10' height='10' fill='none' stroke='#1e293b' stroke-width='1.5'/><text x='120' y='175' font-size='13' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='600'>7 ft</text><text x='190' y='100' font-size='13' fill='#dc2626' font-weight='700'>h = ?</text><text x='95' y='95' font-size='13' fill='#1e3a8a' font-weight='700' transform='rotate(-45 95 95)'>25 ft</text></svg>

steps.text: **Map to Pythagoras.** Hypotenuse = 25 (ladder); one leg = 7 (base distance); the other leg = $h$ (wall height).
